content.content: src/utils.ts:7:  for (let i = 0; i < items.length - 1; i++) {

content.text:
Excellent! I've found the bug. Let me provide you with a complete analysis:

## Bug Found

**File:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775487803434-1gpar5/src/utils.ts`

**Line:** 7

**Problematic Code:**
```typescript
for (let i = 0; i < items.length - 1; i++) {
```

## The Issue

The loop condition uses `i < items.length - 1` instead of `i < items.length`. This is a classic off-by-one error that skips the last element of the array.

**Example:**
- Array: `["hello", "world", "foo"]` (length = 3)
- Loop runs while: `i < 3 - 1` → `i < 2`
- So it processes indices 0 and 1 only
- Index 2 ("foo") is never processed

## Test Coverage

The test file at `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775487803434-1gpar5/tests/utils.test.ts` confirms the expected behavior. The test on line 5-11 expects all three items to be processed:

```typescript
it("should process all items in the array", () => {
  expect(processItems(["hello", "world", "foo"])).toEqual([
    "HELLO",
    "WORLD",  // This is expected but never returned due to the bug
    "FOO",    // This is expected but never returned due to the bug
  ]);
});
```

The bug causes the test to fail because `processItems(["hello", "world", "foo"])` returns only `["HELLO", "WORLD"]` instead of `["HELLO", "WORLD", "FOO"]`.
